title: Star Doll Closet
summary: A little wardrobe for 18" dolls, with a hanging rod, a shelf, and doors — cut a star into the door fronts for the finishing touch kids love.
description:
This is a proper miniature wardrobe scaled for American Girl or other 18" dolls: a 1x12 box with a shelf and a dowel closet rod inside, two doors on the front, and a cute jigsaw-cut star as the signature detail. It's small, cheap, and a great gift — but it has real casework in it (doors, hinges, a rod), so it teaches more than it looks like.

The one measurement to verify before cutting is your 1x10's actual width. The design assumes 9-1/4"; if yours differs, the shelf and top change. The math the source gives is worth following: shelf width is the 1x10 width times two plus a half inch, and the top is that plus another 1-1/2". Get this right and the box goes together square.

The doors are cut 1/4" shorter than the opening for clearance and hung on inset (non-mortise) hinges, with a small clasp to keep them shut — a doll closet gets opened and closed constantly. The star is genuinely easy: draw it with a compass to get the angles even, then cut it out with a jigsaw making straight cuts and clean the edges up with a file. Practice the star on a scrap first so the real one comes out crisp.

tools.note: Cuts the star in the door fronts.

materials.name: 1x10 board, 6 ft
materials.note: Doors. Verify actual width before cutting.

materials.name: Inset cabinet hinges
materials.note: Euro-style butt non-mortise for full overlay/inset.

cutList.note: Width = 1x10 width x2 + 1/2"; verify against your stock.
Cut 1/4" less than the opening height for clearance.

steps.title: Check the 1x10 width and build the box
steps.body: Measure your 1x10's actual width. If it's 9-1/4" you're set; otherwise adjust the cabinet. The shelf width is that width times two plus a half inch, and the top is that plus another 1-1/2". Build the box with countersunk 2" screws and glue (or pocket screws), setting the shelf where you like — screwed in place or on shelf pins for adjustability. Adjust for square.

steps.title: Add the back
steps.body: Cut the 1/4" backerboard to fit and attach it, using it to pull the cabinet square as you fasten.

steps.title: Add the moulding and closet rod
steps.body: If you're using moulding, add it to the top edge and the bottom with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue. Inside, mount the rod pockets and set the dowel closet rod so the doll's clothes can hang.

steps.title: Cut and hang the doors
steps.body: Cut the doors 1/4" less than the overall opening height for clearance and hang them on inset (non-mortise) hinges; standard face-mount hinges also work. Add a clasp or hasp to keep them shut — a doll closet gets played with constantly.

steps.title: Cut the star and finish
steps.body: Draw the star with a compass so the angles come out even — practice on a scrap first. Cut it from the door fronts with a jigsaw using straight cuts, then file the edges smooth. Sand the whole cabinet and paint or stain it.

images.alt: Small wooden doll closet with doors, a hanging rod, and a cut-out star
